g.data-narration: Welcome to learning about storing words in memory. This operation transfers data from CPU registers back to memory locations.
A Store operation transfers data from a processor register to a specified memory location, the reverse of fetching data.
g.text: A Store (Write) operation transfers a word of data
from a processor register to a memory location.

g.data-narration: The store operation uses MAR to hold the address, MDR to hold data, and control signals to manage the write process.
The store sequence follows three transfer steps: address to MAR, data to MDR, then memory writes the data.
First, the processor places the target memory address into MAR - either from the instruction or calculated by the ALU.
Second, the processor copies the data to be stored from the source register into the Memory Data Register.
Third, the processor activates the Write control signal on the control bus to initiate the memory write operation.
Memory reads the address from the address bus and data from the data bus, then stores the data at the specified location.
After writing, memory sends a Memory Function Complete signal back to the processor, confirming the operation finished successfully.
The store operation typically requires three clock cycles: address/data load, write signal, and memory write completion.
Aligned stores to addresses multiple of word size complete in one operation, while unaligned stores may require two operations.
Big-endian stores the most significant byte at the lowest address, while little-endian stores the least significant byte first.
Store operations execute in the MEM stage of the instruction pipeline, calculating addresses and writing data to memory.
Remember: Store transfers data register to memory using MAR, MDR, Write signal, and MFC acknowledgment.
g.text: 1. Store transfers data from CPU register to memory: [LOC] ← R
2. MAR holds target address, MDR holds data to write
3. Write signal issued; waits for MFC confirmation
4. Aligned stores preferred; endianness affects byte ordering
5. Store operations occur in MEM stage (no WB stage)
